sentences.text: We-- we were frightened.
They-- they-- we felt betrayed.
We felt-- That-- that-- we felt depressed, we felt scared.
We were afraid that-- of what's going to happen next.
And we realized that it's going to get a lot worse, but just had no way out.
I remember we tried to-- our youngsters-- as youngsters, we-- the people that we belonging to these organizations, we still were able to get together on weekends.
And we were discussing the situation and there was some kind of talk of an underground in our town.
And I was approached by a friend of mine was contacted and we were discussing-- we-- we-- we were-- we tried to find ways of how to get out of the situation, realizing there's just no way out.
But yet how can we let them slaughter us?
How can they-- how can we let them treat us the way they're treating us?
Constant departures of young people without knowing where they are going, where they were shipped, where they were sent.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- this-- this-- this man, this friend of mine, very good friend of mine, very intelligent, I respected him a lot because he was a very intelligent young man.
And at one time we were discussing it.
He says, Joe, he says, we ought to join this group.
They're trying to organize some contacts with the underground.
They're going to give us ammunition and we'll go into the woods.
We're going to be dying like dogs anyway, we might as well fight for our rights and for our honor.
And maybe we will escape, maybe we'll be able to do something.
And this went on for weeks.
And one day he says, Joe, are you coming?
I decided, I'm going to join them.
I couldn't help realizing how he was right, he was so rational.
It was the only way to do for us youngsters.
Well, if we were sick and old, it's a different story.
But yet, I was worried what will my father say, what will my mother say?
How can I just leave my father and mother?
We were trying to cling together.
We-- they just deprived us of our-- they-- they dehumanized us.
They spit on us, they ridiculed us.
I remember the-- I remember the-- the political commissar, the [GERMAN], who was in charge of our town.
He was like the commissar, the political commissar, for the Nazis.
And he was known.
He was like-- like-- the-- the-- Ivan the Terrible.
And [GERMAN] is the German title for him.
He had a son who was 12 or 13 years old and this son worked within the ghetto and he simply terrorized people, knocked people down for no reason at all.
And as word got around, we naturally looked for him and tried to stay away, stay out of his sight.
One time I'm walking the street and accidentally I happened to come across him and I didn't go out of his way and he knocked me down, right on the street.
A punk of 12, 13 years old.
I was-- I was about 18 years old.
And then I realized that all the rumors that we are hearing is true.
The situation, the moral situation, was absolutely down.
The fear is what got the most of us.
But yet, we were happy to still be together, like in my particular case, with my family.
We went to work and we tried to make the best of it.
But as time went on, they have given out edicts that the whole town has to come-- this was in late, I guess, 1942, when they already were well-established.
They already have conquered all of Poland and they were surging into Russia.
Then they were trying to deal with the Jews.
In 1942-- --it was in the spring of '42, they have organized this big selection.

And it was called the punkt-- the punkt means point-- where they have summoned all the-- by summoned, they have-- they have made plenty clear that everybody that is not going to report-- all the Jews, that are not going to report on this date to this place are going to be shot.
Of course, there were some heroes who tried to do it and they were shot.
Imagine thousands of people, a whole town, being ordered into one big football field, which was at the outskirts of the town.
And there was the Jewish football field.
And that selection took about three days.
This was absolutely an unforgettable experience and just talking about it makes me freeze.
We were thousands of people, naturally our family and everybody.
And in the morning, it was a sunny spring day, I remember.
And the sun was shining.
And as we walked, from all the streets, all these roads led to one main street leading to this football field.
And it was just a sight I had never seen before-- thousands of kids, old people, sick people, lame and blind people.
All the people were streaming to this here.
And then the whole place was surrounded by Nazis with machine gun-- guns and dogs.
And at one point, one center on this football field, there were these Nazis and there was this famous selection, the first one.
There were selections afterwards, but I'll forget this selection.

We were trying to huddle together, and at night the first day, the first night, there was a downpour and everybody was just sitting on the ground getting soaking wet while they were taking a break of the selection.
During the daytime the next morning, the selection started again.
And it was just a simple nod with the thumb, left and right.
Left, you were loaded on trucks and the trucks were hauling away the people, and the right you were separated from families.
There hardly has been one family that did not lose anybody in this selection.
There were older people that were immediately shown to the left, there were young kids-- kids were taken away from their mothers and so forth.
And the screaming and the panic.
No food, no water, two days or so.
After two or two and a half days, when the selection was finished, those people that were selected to be alive were finally let go and there was not a house.
And as we came home and we heard the screamings from our neighbors.
We, fortunately, were lucky to be the whole, our whole family coming back, all five of us.
But not one neighbor didn't left-- didn't lose anybody at this selection.
And we know very well, we had no illusions about it, although all kinds of rumors were circulating at that time.
And there were obviously false rumors that these people are being resettled.

And there were rumors that there's new territories being prepared for them and they're going to be given new lodgings and it's a war and this is near the front and people are getting killed in the war, Germans are getting killed and Poles are getting killed, therefore we have to clear out these cities.
We knew very well that these people are being hauled away to no return places.
Next door to us, the father and mother were taken away.
There were a bunch of kids, young and older kids, about five or six kids, and the father and mother were selected.
I mean, the kids came home to the apartment and-- and-- and it was just unbelievable.
And this went on for days until we-- really, it soaked in.
It really made us aware that we are doomed to-- to extinction, to-- to really being done away with.
But we just couldn't get-- we couldn't-- we just could not believe that this is really happening.
And this went on and on, but this big selection was the real final jolt for us to realize it.
It was right after this here that this friend of mine that I started talking before insisted that I go with him and join up the underground.
And I was afraid, as I said before, and I stayed back.
He went and we later found out that there-- this was a trap that was put on.
And there was about 100 or so, or 200-- it was a big group of young people who had the courage and guts to try to go into the underground.
And they were shot like dogs in the woods.

They were taken out to the woods and they were told this is going to be their territory and from here they're going to start sabotaging the German support-- we've got to do something, we can't just sit like this here and see our mothers and fathers being taken away to wherever.
We didn't know of crematoriums at that time.
We knew we will never see them again.
It was a-- it was a very rational.
But there-- it was a trap, unfortunately.
And I can see his face.
And I-- every time I talk about it, I get choked up.
I can see his face and we argued so fiercely.
That was the part-- there was an underground, there were attempts to resist the Germans, but unfortunately, they were wiped out.
Life in the houses and the apartments was-- became unbearable.
Hunger and deprivation, they dehumanized us.
They degraded us, they-- they made a joke of us.
They grabbed people on the street and they cut off their beards.
They made us lay down and do all kinds of acts of unspeakable things and we had to live with it.
And then after this big selection, there were smaller selections.
But then they realized that it's too much of a-- an ordeal.

So they have given out lists and the Jewish [GERMAN] commander, the council, through the militia, through the Jewish militia-- who were wearing special arm bands and special white hats-- of course, needless to say that we had to wear the Star of David on every garment that we were wearing.
And once you were caught without it, you were severely punished or shot.
For the slightest transgression, you were shot.
And this happened every day on the street, so everybody tried to obey and be a good person, not to get shot.
Yeah.
They issued lists and those lists, they had to deliver to so many people.
[INAUDIBLE].
These people were hauled away to the labor camps.

These-- these labor camps were an arm of the Wehrmacht, which is the German Army, and there were some of them worked in munition factories, some of them worked in other installations which were vital for the war-- German war machine.
And later on, these very same people built the crematories, built Auschwitz, Oswiencim.
These very same labor camps built Auschwitz.
Now, Auschwitz was a small town, it's called Oswiencim and it spells O-S-W-I-E-N-C-I-M, Oswiencim.
The Germans Germanized-- they-- they-- they changed all the Polish names into German.
Like Bendin became Bensberg.
Bensberg.
Oswiencim became Auschwitz.
This town was about 40 kilometers from Bendin, and for some-- I don't know for why reason, for what reason, they built these big camps.
First it was a labor camp and then they build the crematoriums.
And Brzezinka, Birkenau-- Birkenau, which is three kilometers from Auschwitz, was a little town called Brzezinka, B-R-Z-E-Z-I-N-K-A.
It was named Birkenau, B-I-R-K-E-N-A-U, Birkenau.
Birkenau was actually the death factory.
Auschwitz was a labor camp that was converted into a concentration camp.
Naturally, this was not the only camp and-- but in our area, this happened to be the camp.
Later on, it become one of the most concentrated, so much so that 1 and 1/2 million people were burned in Auschwitz. 1 and 1/2 million people, you're talking about millions.

We're losing the fact that these were individuals, human beings with their hopes and prayers and anticipations, people-- old people, young people-- --all kinds of scholars, all kinds of scientists who could have made the world a better place to live.
Auschwitz in itself killed 1 and 1/2 million people.
Now, of course, there was a revision that first it was thought to be 4 million people, but later on in the later years, they really established that it was 1 and 1/2 million people.
And our hometown people were sent to Auschwitz because it was the closest.
But Auschwitz absorbed Romanian people and French people and Holland people and Lithuanian people.
And all of European countries somehow were sent to Auschwitz.
We were still together until the end of 1942.
In 1942, I happened to be on the list.
As we found out that I'm on the list, we went into hiding.
We were hoping against hope.
We were trying, maybe we can escape it.
I knew that they were looking for me, so we went into hiding.
And this was a very good hiding place-- we thought, anyway.
It was in one of the apartments of a relative, of an uncle of mine, and it was a trap door and over the trap door there was a bed.
Underneath the trap door was a storage area for coal.
We used coal at that time, naturally.
It was quite primitive.
Life was primitive to life we know today, but that's all we knew and it was home.
It was sweet home.
We-- my whole family went into this cellar.
In that cellar, you couldn't stand up.
It was about 4 foot high or so.
It was dark, naturally.
It was coal, storage of coal.
We went into the cellar for 1 and 1/2 days or 2 days.
Because if my family didn't go in, if I wasn't there, they would have taken my brother or they would have taken my father or they would have taken my mother.
So the whole family went into hiding.
And we were sitting there and somebody gave us candles, at night, naturally.
And we tried to-- and they brought us food, they delivered us food.
And I feel so depressed that my mother and my father have to lay in this cellar because of me.
I felt guilty.
And one day-- one day-- the next day, I-- I had a shirt and I didn't have any buttons.
And I said, just in case if they catch me, I want to have a button so I can button my shirt.
I was very particular.
And I snuck out and I went into the door of the apartment and looked out in the hallway.
There was nobody in the hallway.
It was a long hallway, the main entrance, between the front gate and the backyard.
And I dashed through that-- --hallway and I went out of my apartment, and apparently, one of the guys was standing in the front and the back.
They came in and they took me.
When they took me I went to my father and my mother and told them to come out.
And it was one of the militia guys that I did know.
He wasn't exactly a close friend, but a guy that knew me.
He knew me personally.
And my mother came out and cried.
And my father came out and my brother came out and my sister came out.
And I can't talk about these moments because I-- I just can't finish it.
This-- these sights and these memories are with me all the time.
And ironically, they are getting more bolder as older as I get.
I keep thinking of them more than I was thinking when I was younger-- 20 years ago, 30 years ago.
I can't talk about these things.
They took me away and my sister came running.
With the truck, I remember the truck taking a sharp turn.
I fell down in an open truck.
They took me to the-- --center where they picked up all the people that they were supposed to they had on the list to deliver.
And my sister came running with her arms stretched and this is the last I saw of my sister.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGH] And I was taken away and that's when camp life started.
I wanted to say something before.
It just escaped me.
It just escaped me.
I-- I didn't want to get off the track, but it will probably come back to me later on.
My brother was taken after me.
There were-- later on I found out-- of course, my mother landed up in Auschwitz, I know that, and my sister landed up in Auschwitz.

And I was told by neighbors who were on the same transport with my mother and sister-- --that survived and I met them later in Israel and they gave me an account of what they have known about my mother and sister.
My mother was selected in Auschwitz to be in a barrack to be gassed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister tried to get in and they didn't let her.
One day until my mother was hauled away to the crematorium, she went-- my sister went in voluntarily.
She wanted to be with her mother.
She didn't want to be without her mother.
My sister was 16 years of age.
She could have very well survived like all the young people that survived by sheer strength of youth.
But she did it, that's what my neighbors related to me.
My sister went voluntarily to be with her mother.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I don't know anything about my father.
I don't know where he disappeared, whether he did get to Auschwitz, whether he was shot or burned.
All I know is I remember the last sight he cried to me, he says, I understand but what am I going to do with your mother?
These are the last words he said to me.
He said, look, I understand.
But how can I explain-- how can I live with your mother after you're gone?
This is what we live with and this is with us all our lives.
I never spoke about these things.
I shunned talking about it years ago when I first came here after the liberation and I tried to acclimatize and I-- to-- to adapt myself to the new life.
Every time I was in company, people would ask me, would you mind telling us?
And I would say, would you mind if we skip the subject?
It was too painful, it was too far from being-- we didn't realize, we didn't-- maybe-- maybe history needs a perspective.
You have to distance yourself to see how grave or how deep the hole or how tall the-- the misfortune is to get the perception, to get the perspective of it.
I didn't want to talk about it because it was like opening up old wounds.
It was like grappling with unhealed sores.
We were too preoccupied with rebuilding our lives, we were-- we were busy building families.
It wasn't an easy feat to acclimatize oneself without knowing the language.
Working, I went to work in a shoe factory because I thought I had some experience.
And I worked hard for very little money.
I wasn't married, I was all by myself.
The first camp was Antonienhutte, Hubertushutte.
They are not really of any relevance.
They are transition camps.
People were picked up here and they were shipped to these camps and then from there, they were shipped to another camp and to another camp.
I went through about three or four-- Antonienhutte, Hubertushutte, Sacrau, and so forth until we wind-- wound up in Blechhammer.
Well, if you want me to spell them, I'll spell them for you.
Let me see.
A-N-T-O-N-I-E-N-H-U-T-T-E, Antonienhutte.
Hutte means a mine.
Antonien is the name.
Antonienhutte and Hubertus.
Hubertus-- H-U-B-E-R-T-U-S.
Hutte, Hubertushutte.
Sacrau, S-A-C-R-A-U, Sacrau.
And there was another one which I don't remember, but four of f them.
But this was like a week here or a week here, three or four days here, three or four days there.
And then we wound up in Blechhammer.
Now, Blechhammer, B-L-E-C-H-A-M-M-E-R.
There was a big plant, a working camp, at the time.
That camp had later on about over 2,000 inmates.
But the location was near a big synthetic gasoline plant where the German-- Germans-- which was very important for the German war machinery.
They were supplying the synthetic gas for the Luftwaffe.
The Luftwaffe is the Air Force, the German Air Force.
So it was a strategic point.
And there were thousands of other inmates.
There were prisoners of war from England, from Canada that we met on the plant.
From Blechhammer, I went-- was shipped to Annaburg, A-N-N-A-B-U-R-G, Annaburg.
There, this Annaburg was a camp where we were segregating or separating the clothes they were taking away from the ghettos and from the camps.
Naturally, as soon as you got into the camp, you had to divest yourself of all the belongings.
I remember I had-- I had-- I was artistically inclined.
I used to draw at home and I had a little album that I drew on of Jewish types, rabbis, and it was bound in a little book.
And this was my dearest possession.
I held onto it.

And one of the transition camps, we came and we had to unpack everything in a big appelplatz, on a roll call plat-- naturally, life in camp consists of getting up in the morning, but this is a whole chapter by itself.
But you asked me where I was going from one place to the other.
Do you want to come back-- what life consisted of in the camp?
I'll tell you.
I was shipped to Annenburg.
Annenburg, we were segregating shoes and coats and clothes and also shipping whatever was usable for the Army, for the Germans.
From Annenburg, I was shipped back to Blechhammer after a couple of months.
It was the wintertime of '43 and it was so bitter cold.
And we were staying on this [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] was a roll call place.
As soon as we get up in the morning-- we'll come to that later.
I went back to Blechhammer.
Blechhammer was a long time there, till the evacuation, January 18, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Russians were pushing westward and Auschwitz-- Blechhammer was a-- a branch of Auschwitz, it was a satellite of Auschwitz.
So all the camps, all the surrounding camps around Auschwitz-- which Blechhammer was was one of them-- had to be evacuated towards the west.
So that was the first big death march that took place, as far as I'm concerned.
January the 18th, 1945.
Up until then, I was in Blechammer.
That much lasted for about a week to 10 days.
This just is impossible to describe-- no food and no clothes.
We took the blankets that we had that we covered ourselves in camp and we walked, thousands of us-- --on their way through Gross-Rosen, which was another concentration camp, Gross-Rosen.
From Gross-Rosen, we were shipped by cattle cars to Buchenwald.
From Buchelwald, I was shipped to Speichingen, which is S-P-E-I-C-H-I-N-G-E-N.
And from Speichingen to Dachau.
Speichingen was a torturous camp, a camp-- a camp just for torture.
There was these lime shafts and we had to dig lime shafts.
And God punished us on top of it.
It was a severe winter of '45, this was April '45, and we were just standing there naked and barefoot with-- and deep-- deep shafts.
And we had to dig out-- there were-- there were these rail cars on top and there were different levels and we had to load lime or-- or-- or-- this was just a tortuous thing.
And from there to Dachau and from Dachau on the way to the Tyrols, there were specific orders by Himmler to be shot, all these inmates.
But fortunately, the American Amy circled and circled these transports and we were liberated on the way somewhere near Garmisch in Bavaria on April 29, 1945.
This was the [GERMAN] and the concentration camps.
